summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, Democracy / anti-fascism, Games / criticism. new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.
